fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need a small runtime value inspection utility in Node.js. basically the idea is: given any JS value, we want a single consistent way to ask 'what is this thing?' and 'what does it look like when you squish it into a bool/number/string?' and also 'can you make this an object for me?'. right now every team that touches a dynamic payload is rolling their own typeof ladder and they keep getting different answers for edge cases — like one service thinks an empty array is truthy-ish numeric zero and another forgets that typed arrays are also objects, it's a mess.

the entry point should read a JSON command off stdin and print results line by line. the value description format is kind of like what we did for the sandbox descriptor protocol in that auth boundary work — same neutral tagging idea, just applied here.

for the object categories, remember the 'object' bucket should be broad — things like arrays, buffers, functions, promises all count. null does NOT. and functions are special in two ways simultaneously.

for boxing, primitives get wrapped fresh (no same reference), everything else comes back as-is. we also need a test runner script under rcb_tests/ that saves raw stdout per case into namespaced files.

please keep the classifier, coercion logic, and I/O adapter in separate logical units — no god files. errors should never bubble raw exceptions to stdout.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: classify(value): Record<string, boolean> | coercePrimitive(value): {boolean, number, string} | box(value): {sameReference, resultTypeof, unwrapped?}

trigger_keywords: which order do the type labels appear
what sequence are the categories printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fixed output order for classify is exactly: undefined, null, boolean, number, string, symbol, array, arraybuffer, typedarray, object, function, promise, dataview — one `<category>=<true|false>` line each, always all 13 lines regardless of the value.

The `object` category is intentionally broad: array, arraybuffer, typedarray, plain object, function, promise, and dataview all report `object=true`. Critically, `null` reports `object=false` — it is NOT treated as an object despite the classic JS quirk. Primitives (undefined, boolean, number, string, symbol) also report `object=false`.

A function value reports BOTH `function=true` AND `object=true` simultaneously. All other specific-object categories (array, arraybuffer, typedarray, promise, dataview) similarly report their own category as true AND `object=true`, while all non-relevant categories remain false.

typedarray and dataview are distinct categories. A typedarray reports `typedarray=true` and `object=true` but `dataview=false` and `arraybuffer=false`. A dataview reports `dataview=true` and `object=true` but `typedarray=false` and `arraybuffer=false`. Neither reports `arraybuffer=true` even though both are backed by a buffer internally. An arraybuffer only reports `arraybuffer=true` (plus `object=true`).

Number coercion rules: undefined→NaN, null→0, false→0, true→1, numeric string→parsed value, non-numeric string→NaN, empty array→0, objects/functions/promises/arraybuffers/typedarray/dataview→NaN. NaN is rendered literally as the string `NaN` in output.

trigger_keywords: what does array coerce to string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: String coercion output: undefined→`undefined`, null→`null`, true→`true`, false→`false`, numbers→decimal text, string→itself, empty array→`` (empty string), typedarray over 12-byte zero buffer→`0,0,0`, arraybuffer→`[object ArrayBuffer]`, plain object→`[object Object]`, promise→`[object Promise]`, function→`function () {}`, dataview→check via `String(new DataView(...))` which yields `[object DataView]`.

Falsey values (boolean coercion → false): undefined, null, boolean false, number 0, empty string. Everything else is truthy: non-zero numbers, non-empty strings, any object-like value (arrays, functions, promises, buffers, typed arrays, plain objects, dataviews).

For `op=box` on a primitive (boolean, number, string): output `same_reference=false`, then `result_typeof=object`, then `unwrapped=<originalValue>`. The wrapper's valueOf returns the original primitive. For undefined/null, behavior is implementation-defined but they are primitives so same_reference=false applies. Symbols similarly.

For `op=box` on a value already an object (array, arraybuffer, typedarray, object, function, promise, dataview): output `same_reference=true`, then `result_typeof=<object|function>`. No `unwrapped` line is emitted. Only a function yields `result_typeof=function`; all other object-like values yield `result_typeof=object`.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts `--cases-dir <subdir>` (default: `test_cases`). For each case it writes raw stdout to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename-stem>@<zero-padded-index>.txt` — e.g. case index 0 of `feature1_type_classification.json` with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` → `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_type_classification@000.txt`. Output files contain ONLY the raw stdout, no PASS/FAIL metadata.

Any malformed or unsupported command must be rendered as a neutral `error=<category>` line to stdout. No host-language exception type, stack trace, or runtime message suffix may appear in stdout. The process must not crash visibly — all errors are caught and converted to the error line format.

Supported value kinds in the JSON `value` descriptor: `undefined`, `null`, `boolean` (data: true|false), `number` (data: number), `string` (data: string), `symbol` (data: string label), `array` (empty), `arraybuffer` (byteLength: n), `typedarray` (byteLength: n — Int32Array over zero-filled buffer), `object` (empty plain object), `function` (source: `function () {}`), `promise` (pending), `dataview` (byteLength: n — DataView over n-byte buffer).

The codebase must have distinct logical units: (1) core type classifier — no I/O dependency, (2) core coercion logic — no I/O dependency, (3) execution/IO adapter — reads stdin JSON, calls core, writes stdout. The adapter depends on the core; the core must not depend on the adapter. This is enforced by the SRP/DIP constraints in the PRD.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'classify', 'coerce_primitive', 'box'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same neutral tagging idea, just applied here — like what we did for the sandbox descriptor protocol in that auth boundary work
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON `value` descriptor format with a `kind` field plus optional `data`/`byteLength` fields, as fully specified in the PRD's 'value describes the runtime value to construct' section under Core Features

context_pointers.vague_reference: the 'object' bucket should be broad — things like arrays, buffers, functions, promises all count
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `object` category in classify output returns true for: array, arraybuffer, typedarray, plain object, function, promise, dataview — but explicitly false for null and all primitives

context_pointers.vague_reference: functions are special in two ways simultaneously
context_pointers.actual_meaning: A function reports both `function=true` AND `object=true` in the classify output, AND in box output it reports `result_typeof=function` (not `object`) while still being `same_reference=true`

Output files go to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/` with naming pattern `<filename-stem>@<zero-padded-3-digit-index>.txt`, namespaced by the --cases-dir argument so different case directories never overwrite each other

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please refer to the PRD spec in start.md and the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact contract. If something still seems ambiguous, check how the existing test cases exercise that behavior and match it exactly.

feature2_primitive_coercion.json.description: Coerce a runtime value simultaneously to a truthiness boolean, to a number (NaN when not numerically representable), and to its string form, returning all three coercions for one input.

feature3_object_boxing.json.description: Convert a runtime value to an object: primitives are boxed into a fresh wrapper object whose unwrapped value equals the original primitive, while values that are already objects (including functions) are returned as the very same reference.
